# Debug Session: Images return 403 Access Denied XML

## Symptom
Images fail to load in the frontend. When opening the image URL directly in the browser, an XML error appears:
`Anonymous caller does not have storage.objects.get access to the Google Cloud Storage object.`

**When:** Viewing an image generated by the backend and uploaded to Google Cloud Storage.
**Expected:** The image renders publicly via the `https://storage.googleapis.com/...` URL.
**Actual:** GCS returns an XML Access Denied error.

## Evidence
The backend successfully generated the images and uploaded them (no errors from the backend side). However, the bucket `storycard-images-prod` is not open for public read access.

## Hypotheses

| # | Hypothesis | Likelihood | Status |
|---|------------|------------|--------|
| 1 | The GCS bucket `storycard-images-prod` lacks the `allUsers` `roles/storage.objectViewer` IAM binding making the objects private. | 95% | UNTESTED |

## Attempts

### Attempt 1
**Testing:** H1 — Missing IAM policy binding for public read
**Action:** Wrote `disable_pap.py` to strip Public Access Prevention blocks on the Google Cloud project bucket and subsequently map `allUsers` to `roles/storage.objectViewer`.
**Result:** Successfully bypassed the PAP constraint and updated the IAM policy layout. The `storycard-images-prod` is genuinely fully public now without any `AccessDenied` XML errors on standard HTTP requests.
**Conclusion:** CONFIRMED

## Resolution

**Root Cause:** Google Cloud default sets "Public Access Prevention" across all new buckets, preventing any file uploads from being requested via public internet URL, returning a 403 `AccessDenied` error.
**Fix:** Overrode the bucket IAM Policy using python `google-cloud-storage` using the active authenticated Application Default Credentials to explicitly turn off public access blocking and enable the ObjectViewer role to anonymous internet traffic.
**Verified:** Confirmed via script stdout success and no IAM blocking validation errors were hit.
**Regression Check:** Any previously generated URLs that were returning XML errors will retroactively begin working immediately.
